mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

frontmatter.title: Path objects and the univalence axiom

mainmatter.p: A reflexive graph is given by a type A together with a family of types \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to A \to \mathsf {Type} and an assignment 1_{ x } : x \cong x to each x,y: A. The reflexive graph is called univalent when for all x:A the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y is a proposition, and so is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {}.
We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

The intrinsic path object on a type A, written \mathord { \vartriangle } A, is defined by setting x \cong _{ \mathord { \vartriangle } A } y : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x = y \right ) \mathclose {} and 1_{ x } : \equiv \mathsf {refl}.
That the reflexive graph defined above is univalent follows immediately by identification induction.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.

Given a dependent function f \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, if each component Bx is contractible with centre fx then the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} is contractible with centre f.
This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, it suffices to show any other g \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products () shows that \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is contractible with centre f.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. We equip the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} with the structure of a path object, setting f \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}} g : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx \cong _{Bx}gx and 1_{ f } : \equiv \lambda x \mathpunct {.} 1_{ fx }.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products () via the distributivity of \prod over \sum.

Let A,B \colon \mathsf {PathObj} be two path objects. The binary product A \times B is equipped with the structure of a path object by setting \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{A \times B} \mathopen {} \left ( u,v \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv x \cong _{A}u \times y \cong _{B}v and 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} } : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {refl} , \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {}.
The univalence property of A \times B follows immediately from that of A and B.

If f \colon A \to B has a quasi-inverse g \colon B \to A, then a path object structure on A induces a path object structure on B, setting \mathopen {} \left ( x \cong _B y \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( gx \cong _A gy \right ) \mathclose {}.

We first prove the lemma for biinvertible functions, so that both the section and retraction are tracked by g. In this case, we may use singleton induction via the univalence principle for types to restrict attention to the case that f is the identity map, and the result is immediate.

Let A be a path object, and let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1 \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \right ) \mathclose {} \to A creates a path object structure \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} on \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx as follows:

ol.li: we have \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to x \cong _A y;
the reflexivity datum 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} } is given by 1_{ x } in A;
univalence follows from univalence of A and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

mainmatter.p: An equivalence from A to B is defined to be a function A \to B equipped with a biinverse.

A reflexive graph is given by a type A together with a family of types \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to A \to \mathsf {Type} and an assignment 1_{ x } : x \cong x to each x,y: A. The reflexive graph is called univalent when for all x:A the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y is a proposition, and so is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {}.
We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

If f \colon A \to B has a quasi-inverse g \colon B \to A, then a path object structure on A induces a path object structure on B, setting \mathopen {} \left ( x \cong _B y \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( gx \cong _A gy \right ) \mathclose {}.

We first prove the lemma for biinvertible functions, so that both the section and retraction are tracked by g. In this case, we may use singleton induction via the univalence principle for types to restrict attention to the case that f is the identity map, and the result is immediate.

For any precategory C, the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( D : \mathsf {Precategory} \right ) \mathclose {} } } {D \cong C} of isomorphisms of precategories onto C is contractible.

The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{ C } \colon C \cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type} \\ D_1&:D_0 \to D_0 \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_0&: D_0 \to C_0 \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \to C \mathopen {} \left ( F_0x , F_0y \right ) \mathclose {} \\ h_1 &: \mathsf {isBiinvertible} \, F_0 \\ h_2 &: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isPropMap} \mathopen {} \left ( F_1xy \right ) \mathclose {} \\ h_3 &: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {isSurjection} \mathopen {} \left ( F_1xy \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
mainmatter.p: A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type} \\ D_1&:D_0 \to D_0 \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_0&: D_0 \cong C_0 \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y: D_0 \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \cong C \mathopen {} \left ( F_0x , F_0y \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
mainmatter.p: By the univalence principle for types, we may contract \mathopen {} \left ( D_0,F_0 \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( \left \lvert C \right \rvert , 1_{ \left \lvert C \right \rvert } \right ) \mathclose {}:
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} D_1&: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_1&: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \in C \right ) \mathclose {} } } D_1xy \cong C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
mainmatter.p: Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (, ):
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} D_1&: \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \left \lvert C \right \rvert \to \mathsf {Set} \\ F_1&: D_1 = \lambda x,y \mathpunct {.} { C \mathopen {} \left ( x , y \right ) \mathclose {} } \end {aligned}
mainmatter.p: By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.
